title: Cellular Technologies
description: Practice identifying cellular network generations from 1G through 5G, comparing GSM and CDMA technologies, understanding LTE architecture, and exploring 5G capabilities including mmWave and sub-6 GHz deployments.
starterCode:
# CELLULAR TECHNOLOGIES EXERCISE

## Part 1: Cellular Generations Overview

1. 1G cellular networks used _______ (analog) voice transmission and were introduced in the 1980s.
2. 2G introduced _______ voice transmission, significantly improving call quality and capacity.
3. The two competing 2G standards were GSM and _______.
4. 3G networks introduced mobile _______ access with speeds up to several Mbps.
5. 3G's enhanced version, HSPA+, could achieve theoretical speeds of up to _______ Mbps.

## Part 2: GSM vs CDMA

6. GSM stands for Global System for Mobile _______.
7. CDMA stands for Code Division Multiple _______.
8. GSM uses a removable _______ card that stores subscriber identity and can be moved between phones.
9. The GSM SIM card contains the _______ (International Mobile Subscriber Identity) number.
10. CDMA does not use SIM cards; instead the phone's identity is stored in the _______ itself.
11. GSM divides channels using _______ Division Multiple Access (TDMA) for separating calls.
12. CDMA assigns a unique _______ to each call, allowing multiple calls on the same frequency simultaneously.
13. Globally, _______ became the dominant 2G/3G standard, used by most carriers worldwide.

## Part 3: 4G LTE

14. LTE stands for Long-Term _______.
15. 4G LTE uses _______ (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) for its downlink transmission.
16. LTE provides theoretical download speeds of up to _______ Mbps (Category 4).
17. LTE-Advanced (LTE-A) uses carrier _______ to combine multiple frequency bands for higher speeds.
18. LTE-A can achieve theoretical download speeds of up to _______ Mbps.
19. VoLTE stands for Voice over _______, enabling HD voice calls over the 4G data network.
20. LTE uses an all-_______ architecture, eliminating the circuit-switched voice network of earlier generations.

## Part 4: 5G Technology

21. 5G NR stands for 5G New _______.
22. 5G operates in three spectrum bands: low-band, mid-band, and _______ (mmWave).
23. mmWave stands for _______ wave, operating at frequencies above 24 GHz.
24. mmWave 5G offers extremely high speeds but has very limited _______ and poor building penetration.
25. Sub-6 GHz 5G provides a balance between _______ and coverage area.
26. 5G targets latency as low as _______ millisecond(s), enabling real-time applications.
27. 5G supports up to _______ million devices per square kilometer for IoT deployments.
28. Network _______ in 5G allows operators to create virtual networks tailored for specific use cases.

## Part 5: Cellular Comparison and Use Cases

29. The transition from 3G/4G to 5G is driven by demand for higher speeds, lower latency, and massive _______ connectivity.
30. MIMO technology in 5G uses _______ antenna arrays with dozens or hundreds of antenna elements.
31. Small _______ are low-power base stations used to extend 5G mmWave coverage in dense urban areas.
32. 5G's ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) mode is designed for applications like autonomous _______ and remote surgery.
33. The cellular technology that unified GSM and CDMA carriers onto a single standard is _______.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Cellular Generations Overview

1. 1G cellular networks used **analog** voice transmission and were introduced in the 1980s.
2. 2G introduced **digital** voice transmission, significantly improving call quality and capacity.
3. The two competing 2G standards were GSM and **CDMA**.
4. 3G networks introduced mobile **broadband** access with speeds up to several Mbps.
5. 3G's enhanced version, HSPA+, could achieve theoretical speeds of up to **42** Mbps.

## Part 2: GSM vs CDMA

6. GSM stands for Global System for Mobile **Communications**.
7. CDMA stands for Code Division Multiple **Access**.
8. GSM uses a removable **SIM** card that stores subscriber identity and can be moved between phones.
9. The GSM SIM card contains the **IMSI** (International Mobile Subscriber Identity) number.
10. CDMA does not use SIM cards; instead the phone's identity is stored in the **handset** itself.
11. GSM divides channels using **Time** Division Multiple Access (TDMA) for separating calls.
12. CDMA assigns a unique **code** to each call, allowing multiple calls on the same frequency simultaneously.
13. Globally, **GSM** became the dominant 2G/3G standard, used by most carriers worldwide.

## Part 3: 4G LTE

14. LTE stands for Long-Term **Evolution**.
15. 4G LTE uses **OFDMA** (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) for its downlink transmission.
16. LTE provides theoretical download speeds of up to **150** Mbps (Category 4).
17. LTE-Advanced (LTE-A) uses carrier **aggregation** to combine multiple frequency bands for higher speeds.
18. LTE-A can achieve theoretical download speeds of up to **1,000** Mbps.
19. VoLTE stands for Voice over **LTE**, enabling HD voice calls over the 4G data network.
20. LTE uses an all-**IP** architecture, eliminating the circuit-switched voice network of earlier generations.

## Part 4: 5G Technology

21. 5G NR stands for 5G New **Radio**.
22. 5G operates in three spectrum bands: low-band, mid-band, and **high-band** (mmWave).
23. mmWave stands for **millimeter** wave, operating at frequencies above 24 GHz.
24. mmWave 5G offers extremely high speeds but has very limited **range** and poor building penetration.
25. Sub-6 GHz 5G provides a balance between **speed** and coverage area.
26. 5G targets latency as low as **1** millisecond(s), enabling real-time applications.
27. 5G supports up to **1** million devices per square kilometer for IoT deployments.
28. Network **slicing** in 5G allows operators to create virtual networks tailored for specific use cases.

## Part 5: Cellular Comparison and Use Cases

29. The transition from 3G/4G to 5G is driven by demand for higher speeds, lower latency, and massive **IoT** connectivity.
30. MIMO technology in 5G uses **massive** antenna arrays with dozens or hundreds of antenna elements.
31. Small **cells** are low-power base stations used to extend 5G mmWave coverage in dense urban areas.
32. 5G's ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) mode is designed for applications like autonomous **vehicles** and remote surgery.
33. The cellular technology that unified GSM and CDMA carriers onto a single standard is **LTE**.
hints: 1G = analog; 2G = digital (GSM/CDMA); 3G = mobile broadband; 4G = LTE (all-IP); 5G = NR (mmWave + sub-6).
GSM uses a removable SIM card with IMSI; CDMA stores identity in the handset — GSM became the global dominant standard.
GSM uses Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA); CDMA assigns unique codes to each call on the same frequency.
LTE = Long-Term Evolution; uses OFDMA for downlink and an all-IP architecture (no circuit-switched voice).
LTE-Advanced uses carrier aggregation to bond multiple frequency bands, boosting speeds up to 1 Gbps.
5G NR (New Radio) operates in low-band, mid-band, and high-band (mmWave) spectrum ranges.
mmWave (millimeter wave) offers multi-Gbps speeds but very short range and poor building penetration.
5G targets 1ms latency, supports 1 million devices/km², and uses network slicing for virtual networks.
Massive MIMO in 5G uses arrays with dozens/hundreds of antenna elements for beamforming and capacity.
Small cells are low-power base stations that extend mmWave coverage; LTE unified GSM and CDMA carriers.

testCases.description: Should correctly explain references sim card concepts and implementation details
